at_a_glance: Iran talks falter as regional and Western capitals assess next steps over the Strait of Hormuz standoff.
EU airlines warn of potential jet fuel shortages if the key waterway remains closed.
Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations across the front.
British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer' amid mounting financial pressure.
At least 30 feared dead after a crush at a Haitian tourist site.
Asha Bhosle, the voice of Bollywood, dies aged 92.

stories.title: Stalemate with Iran raises stakes in Strait of Hormuz as fuel squeeze fears grow
stories.summary: Diplomatic talks with Iran have faltered, increasing uncertainty over the future of shipping and energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz. Airlines and shippers are drawing up contingency plans, while politicians urge restraint and a rapid return to negotiations.
body: A renewed breakdown in talks with Iran has injected fresh volatility into global energy and transport markets, with policymakers warning that any prolonged disruption in the Strait of Hormuz would reverberate well beyond the Gulf. With efforts to find a diplomatic off-ramp stalling, attention has turned to what immediate steps governments and industry can take to keep trade moving and prices in check.

Aviation leaders in Europe have warned of potential jet fuel shortages if access through the strait remains constrained, highlighting the vulnerability of hub airports that rely on steady seaborne supply. Shipping groups have also been urged to resist any new payment demands linked to transit, as insurers and charterers reassess risk premiums and routing options.

In London, Labour leader Keir Starmer has urged Washington and Tehran to ‘find a way through’ the impasse, reflecting wider calls in Western capitals and the region to de-escalate and re-open channels. Diplomats are exploring intermediary-led proposals that could introduce limited confidence-building measures and phased easing of restrictions.

For now, energy traders are braced for volatility, with refiners, airlines and logistics firms stress-testing alternative supply chains, including pipeline backfills and stock draws. Any protracted stand-off would test governments’ emergency fuel arrangements and could feed into broader inflation pressures, even as consumers and businesses continue to grapple with elevated costs.

stories.title: Kyiv and Moscow trade blame over fresh ceasefire breaches
stories.summary: Ukraine and Russia have accused each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations, underscoring the fragility of attempts to cool fighting along the front. The exchanges risk complicating diplomatic efforts and deepen concern for civilians near contested areas.
body: Both Kyiv and Moscow reported a surge in ceasefire violations, with each side alleging breaches by the other across multiple sectors of the front. The claims point to continued use of artillery, drones and small-unit skirmishes that have kept the line of contact volatile despite periodic efforts to reduce hostilities.

The uptick in incidents threatens to undermine already tentative de-escalation efforts and adds to the difficulty of establishing stable humanitarian access. Aid groups say stop-start fighting hampers evacuations and the repair of critical infrastructure, from power networks to water systems, in communities repeatedly caught in the crossfire.

Western governments have called for restraint and renewed engagement through diplomatic channels, but there is little sign of a breakthrough. The military picture remains fluid, with localised advances and withdrawals that have not altered the broader strategic stalemate.

As winter gives way to spring, commanders on both sides are recalibrating positions and logistics, raising the risk of miscalculation. With talks faltering and trust scarce, international mediators face a narrowing window to stabilise the situation before battlefield dynamics harden further.

stories.title: Ministers face summer deadline as British Steel said to need nationalisation
stories.summary: The government is under mounting pressure to intervene after British Steel was reported to need nationalising by the summer to stay afloat. The warning lands as ministers signal a relaxed stance on Chinese car imports, sharpening debate over industrial strategy and competitiveness.
body: British Steel’s finances are said to be deteriorating to the point where only a state takeover before the summer would secure operations and protect jobs, according to people familiar with the situation. The prospect of nationalisation would mark a dramatic turn for a company seen as strategically important to the UK’s manufacturing base and major infrastructure projects.

Officials now face hard choices over how to balance fiscal caution with industrial capacity and regional employment. Any move to nationalise would raise complex questions about long-term investment needs, decarbonisation of heavy industry and the terms on which the state would eventually exit.

At the same time, ministers have signalled a relaxed approach to Chinese car imports, arguing that consumers benefit from lower prices and greater choice. The stance has unsettled parts of the domestic automotive supply chain, which is wrestling with the cost of the electric transition, rules of origin and the global subsidy race.

Business groups and unions are urging a clearer framework that links trade policy, energy costs and green investment to sectoral resilience. Without it, they warn, ad hoc interventions risk becoming costlier while leaving the UK exposed to external shocks and intensifying international competition.

stories.title: Asha Bhosle, the sound of Bollywood, dies aged 92
stories.summary: Asha Bhosle, one of Indian cinema’s most beloved playback singers, has died aged 92. Her seven-decade career, spanning thousands of songs across genres and languages, helped define the soundscape of Bollywood and resonated with audiences worldwide.
body: Asha Bhosle’s voice became a touchstone of Indian popular culture, carrying the stories and emotions of countless films to generations of listeners. Her versatility – from classical-inflected melodies to cabaret numbers and pop-infused duets – set a standard for playback singing and broadened the palette of Hindi cinema.

Born into a family steeped in music, she forged a distinct artistic identity and prolific output that earned admiration across the industry and far beyond India’s borders. Her recordings travelled with the South Asian diaspora and introduced global audiences to the sweep of Bollywood storytelling.

Over decades in studios and on stages, Bhosle collaborated with leading composers and performers, continually reinventing her repertoire while retaining a signature timbre that was instantly recognisable. Tributes from artists and public figures have praised her range, resilience and the cultural bridge her music created.

Her passing draws a line under a singular chapter in film music, but the catalogue she leaves behind remains part of everyday life – from cinema soundtracks and radio staples to wedding playlists and festival stages. For millions, Asha Bhosle’s songs will continue to evoke memory, movement and joy.
